claimed_contribution: The paper identifies conditions under which consumers spontaneously form self-relevant product-use images without explicit imagination instructions.
It proposes and tests that two conditions must co-occur for this process: self-focused attention and contextual cues applicable to the product's normal use situation.
It shows that these effects generalize from product evaluations to actual product choice and clarifies that imagined use, rather than imagination difficulty, drives the effect.
context: The studies are controlled laboratory consumer-judgment tasks with undergraduate participants evaluating common products and, in one study, selecting a take-home gift. Context cues were manipulated as social versus nonsocial incidental music or by having participants evaluate products in the presence versus absence of others.
design_and_data: The authors report three experiments plus preliminary tests. Experiment 1 (n = 188) and Experiment 2 (n = 119) used mixed designs that manipulated self-focused attention (mirror vs. no mirror) and situational context (social vs. nonsocial music; with others vs. alone) while varying whether product descriptions emphasized socially relevant or nonsocial attributes for televisions and apartments, with shampoo as a filler. Measures included product evaluations, imagined self-use, imagination difficulty, self-focus manipulation checks, and affect/context checks; mediation analyses tested whether imagined use explained evaluation effects. Experiment 3 (n = 114) manipulated self-focus and music (social, nonsocial, none) and examined rank-ordered choice of a social product (chips), nonsocial product (tea), or filler (pen) as a free gift.
main_findings: Self-focused attention increased evaluations only for products whose typical usage context matched the current contextual cue: social products under social cues and nonsocial products under nonsocial cues.

The interaction effects on evaluations were mediated by participants' tendency to imagine themselves using the product; when cues were mismatched or self-focused attention was low, this mechanism was not observed.

The same pattern extended to behavior: self-focused attention shifted gift choice toward chips under social music and toward tea under nonsocial music, while alternative accounts based on imagination difficulty, arousal or relaxation, and involvement were not supported.
one_line_summary: Across three lab experiments, self-focused attention increased product evaluations and gift choices only when incidental social versus nonsocial context cues matched the context in which the product is typically used, because matching cues increased imagined self-use.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior consumer imagery and self-referencing research by moving beyond explicit imagination instructions to spontaneous self-use imagery in judgment contexts. It also extends work on incidental music effects by showing that context cues influence evaluations through cue-product applicability and self-focused attention rather than simple affect transfer.
research_gap: Prior imagery studies in consumer research typically instructed participants to imagine product use. The paper argues that the conditions under which consumers spontaneously generate self-use imagery in ordinary judgment settings, and the consequences of that imagery for evaluation, had not been clearly identified.
research_question: The paper asks when consumers spontaneously imagine themselves using a product, without being instructed to do so, and when those imaginings influence judgments. It tests whether self-focused attention and the applicability of situational context cues jointly determine this process.
